text: Mind is the forerunner of all actions. All deeds are led by mind, created by mind. If one speaks or acts with a corrupt mind, suffering follows, as the wheel follows the hoof of an ox.
Mind is the forerunner of all actions. All deeds are led by mind, created by mind. If one speaks or acts with a pure mind, happiness follows, like a shadow that never departs.
Not to do any evil, to cultivate good, to purify one's mind — this is the teaching of the Buddhas.
You yourself must strive. The Buddhas only point the way. Those meditative ones who tread the path are released from the bonds of Mara.
All conditioned things are impermanent. When one sees this with wisdom, one turns away from suffering. This is the path to purification.
All conditioned things are unsatisfactory. When one sees this with wisdom, one turns away from suffering. This is the path to purification.
All things are not-self. When one sees this with wisdom, one turns away from suffering. This is the path to purification.
Conquer anger with non-anger. Conquer badness with goodness. Conquer meanness with generosity. Conquer dishonesty with truth.

source: Samyutta Nikaya 56.11 — Setting the Wheel of Dhamma in Motion
text: Now this, monks, is the noble truth of suffering: birth is suffering, aging is suffering, illness is suffering, death is suffering; union with what is displeasing is suffering; separation from what is pleasing is suffering; not to get what one wants is suffering.

source: Samyutta Nikaya 56.11 — Setting the Wheel of Dhamma in Motion
text: Now this, monks, is the noble truth of the origin of suffering: it is this craving which leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, seeking delight here and there — that is, craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, craving for extermination.

source: Samyutta Nikaya 56.11 — Setting the Wheel of Dhamma in Motion
text: Now this, monks, is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering: it is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the giving up and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance on it.

source: Samyutta Nikaya 56.11 — Setting the Wheel of Dhamma in Motion
text: Now this, monks, is the noble truth of the way leading to the cessation of suffering: it is the Noble Eightfold Path — right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.
When this exists, that comes to be. With the arising of this, that arises. When this does not exist, that does not come to be. With the cessation of this, that ceases.

May all beings be happy and secure; may their hearts be wholesome. Whatever living beings there are — feeble or strong, long or tall, stout or medium, short or small, seen or unseen, near or far, born or to be born — may all beings be happy.

source: Majjhima Nikaya 10 — Satipatthana Sutta (Foundations of Mindfulness)
text: This is the direct path for the purification of beings, for the surmounting of sorrow and lamentation, for the disappearance of pain and grief, for the attainment of the true way, for the realization of Nibbana — namely, the four foundations of mindfulness.

A monk abides contemplating the body as a body, ardent, fully aware, and mindful, having put away covetousness and grief for the world. He abides contemplating feelings as feelings, mind as mind, and mind-objects as mind-objects.

source: Majjhima Nikaya 118 — Anapanasati Sutta (Mindfulness of Breathing)
text: Breathing in long, he discerns that he is breathing in long; breathing out long, he discerns that he is breathing out long. Breathing in short, he discerns that he is breathing in short. He trains himself to breathe in calming the body, and to breathe out calming the body.

source: Samyutta Nikaya 56.11 — Setting the Wheel of Dhamma in Motion
text: Monks, these two extremes ought not to be practiced by one who has gone forth: the pursuit of sensual happiness in sensual pleasures, which is low and vulgar; and the pursuit of self-mortification, which is painful and unworthy. Avoiding both these extremes, the Tathagata has realized the Middle Way.

Beings are owners of their actions, heirs of their actions, born of their actions, related through their actions, and have their actions as their refuge. Whatever action they do, for good or for evil, to that they will fall heir.
Form is emptiness, emptiness is form. Emptiness is not other than form; form is not other than emptiness. In the same way, feeling, perception, mental formations, and consciousness are emptiness.

All dharmas are marked with emptiness: they are not produced or destroyed, not defiled or immaculate, not deficient or complete. Therefore, in emptiness there is no form, no feeling, no perception, no mental formations, no consciousness.
Gate gate paragate parasamgate bodhi svaha. Gone, gone, gone beyond, gone completely beyond — enlightenment! So be it.
All composed things are like a dream, a phantom, a drop of dew, a flash of lightning. That is how to meditate on them, that is how to observe them.

A bodhisattva who creates the perception of a self, a person, a being, or a living soul is not a bodhisattva. The reason is that there is no independently existing object to which the name bodhisattva can be applied.

source: Bodhicaryavatara (The Way of the Bodhisattva), Shantideva, Ch. 8
text: All the suffering in the world arises from seeking pleasure for oneself. All the happiness in the world arises from seeking pleasure for others.
If there is a remedy when trouble strikes, what reason is there for dejection? And if there is no remedy for it, what use is there in being glum?

source: Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind — Shunryu Suzuki
text: In the beginner's mind there are many possibilities, but in the expert's mind there are few. The goal of practice is always to keep our beginner's mind.

source: Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind — Shunryu Suzuki
text: When you do something, you should burn yourself up completely, like a good bonfire, leaving no trace of yourself. This is the way to practice.

source: The Gateless Gate — Mumonkan, Case 1 (Joshu's Dog)
text: A monk asked Joshu: 'Does a dog have Buddha-nature or not?' Joshu answered: 'Mu!' Mumon's comment: To realize Zen, you must pass through the barrier of the patriarchs. Enlightenment always comes after the road of thinking is blocked.

Nansen asked: 'What is the Way?' Joshu said: 'Ordinary mind is the Way.' Nansen said: 'If you try to direct yourself toward it, you move away from it.' Joshu asked: 'If we don't try, how can we know it is the Way?' Nansen said: 'The Way is not about knowing or not knowing.'

source: Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind — Shunryu Suzuki
text: The most important thing is not to be attached to anything. Just sit and observe your breathing. When your mind wanders, gently bring it back. There is no right or wrong in meditation — there is only practice.

source: The Tibetan Book of the Dead — Introduction by Padmasambhava
text: O nobly-born, the time has come for you to seek the path. Your breathing is about to cease. In the past your teacher has set you face to face with the Clear Light; now you are about to experience it in its Reality.

source: The Words of My Perfect Teacher — Patrul Rinpoche
text: This precious human life, free and well-favored, is difficult to obtain. Now that you have the chance to realize the full human potential, if you don't make good use of this opportunity, how could you possibly expect to have such a chance again?

source: Attributed to the Dalai Lama XIV
text: If you want others to be happy, practice compassion. If you want to be happy, practice compassion. Compassion is not religious business, it is human business. It is not luxury, it is essential for our own peace and mental stability.
There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned. If there were not this unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned, no escape would be discerned from what is born, become, made, conditioned.

I am subject to aging, have not gone beyond aging. I am subject to illness, have not gone beyond illness. I am subject to death, have not gone beyond death. All that is mine, beloved and pleasing, will become otherwise, will become separated from me.
